title: Evaluating Retrieval Quality
concept: Measure sentence retrieval using ranking metrics like Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) or Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) against human-labeled queries.

exercises.prompt: What do we need in order to evaluate if our sentence retrieval system is returning correct results?

options.text: The length of the search query in words

exercises.explain: To measure how good a search system is, we compare its outputs to human-labeled answers, which act as the ground truth.

cards.title: Why Order Matters
cards.body: When you search for something online, you want the absolute best answer to be the very first result, not buried under pages of junk. How do developers measure if their search AI is actually delivering the best answers first? Let's find out!

cards.title: Ranking Metrics
cards.body: To evaluate how well an AI retrieves information, we use ranking metrics. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) checks how quickly the first correct answer appears. Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) measures the overall quality of the ranking, giving more points when highly relevant answers are placed right at the top.

cards.title: The Search Engine Baker
cards.body: Imagine asking a smart assistant for a cake recipe. If the absolute best recipe is the very first link it shows you, that is a perfect score! But if the best recipe is buried all the way down on the fifth page of results, the assistant didn't do a very good job. Retrieval metrics measure exactly this: how high up the best answers are placed.

cards.title: Evaluating System A vs B
cards.body:
Let's say a user asks: "How do plants breathe?"
System A puts the perfect answer at Rank 1. (Reciprocal Rank = 1/1 = 1.0)
System B puts the perfect answer at Rank 4. (Reciprocal Rank = 1/4 = 0.25)
By comparing these scores across many user queries, we can scientifically prove that System A is much better at finding information than System B.

cards.title: Myth: All Finds are Equal
cards.body: You might think that simply counting how many correct sentences an AI finds is enough. But if the best sentence is hidden at the bottom of page 10, users will never see it! The position (or rank) of the correct answer is just as important as finding it.

cards.title: Ranking Score Comparison
cards.body: See how the placement of the relevant result changes the score. Higher ranks get exponentially better scores!

left.text: Correct answer is the very first result. Reciprocal Rank score = 1.0 (Excellent!)

right.text: Correct answer is down at the third position. Reciprocal Rank score = 0.33 (Needs improvement!)

exercises.prompt: Why is NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) useful for evaluating search results?

options.text: It only looks at the very first correct answer.
It scores the system based on the order and quality of multiple correct answers.
It only works if there is exactly one correct answer.

exercises.explain: NDCG stands for Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain. It is highly valued because it handles multiple correct answers and penalizes the system if the best answers are placed lower down the list.

exercises.prompt: To calculate metrics like MRR or NDCG, we must compare the AI's search results against human-labeled correct answers.
exercises.explain: Ranking metrics require human-labeled test data (ground truth) so we know which sentences are actually correct and where they should have been ranked.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about search ranking metrics.
exercises.sentence: If we only care about where the first correct answer appears in our list, we use the metric called _______.
exercises.explain: Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) focuses purely on the position of the very first relevant item returned in the list.

exercises.prompt: Identify the option that correctly highlights the difference between AI similarity rankings and human ground-truth labels.

options.text: Option A: "The AI system ranked this sentence first, so it is automatically the absolute human truth."
Option B: "Even though the AI gave this sentence a high similarity score, human testers labeled it as completely unrelated."
Option C: "We don't need human labels because the algorithm calculated the NDCG automatically without data."

exercises.explain: AI systems do not have feelings or preferences. They rank sentences based on mathematical similarity scores, whereas humans decide what is actually useful (ground truth) based on real-world meaning.

exercises.prompt: An AI search engine returns 5 documents for a query. The first correct document is at position (rank) 3. What is the Reciprocal Rank (RR) score for this query?
exercises.explain: MRR only looks at where the single first correct answer is located. Since the correct document is at rank 3, the Reciprocal Rank is 1/3 (or 0.33). The other documents are ignored for this specific metric.
